content.body: Should it be roundtrip or round-trip?

Examples: "roundtripable data", "ensures X and Y roundtrip", "work without a server roundtrip", "go through multiple network roundtrips", "unpaired surrogates will not roundtrip", "in such cases serialize-parse roundtrips work fine", "this algorithm does not round-trip an initial U+000A (LF)", "the markup being round-tripped can be conforming", "an editing tool is allowed to round-trip erroneous content", "For the purposes of round-tripping"

Most of those are using it as a verb, which appears to be a relatively recent usage. I'm not finding much consensus on the spelling. Personally, I think the hyphenless reads slightly better, though it's close enough that I ca. For usage as a noun, I think either (or "round trip") is fine.

* Most of those are using it as a verb, which appears to be a relatively recent usage. I'm not finding much consensus on the spelling. Personally, I think the hyphenless reads slightly better, though it's close enough that I can't be sure. For usage as a noun, I think either (or "round trip") works.

m.new_content.body: Most of those are using it as a verb, which appears to be a relatively recent usage. I'm not finding much consensus on the spelling. Personally, I think the hyphenless reads slightly better, though it's close enough that I can't be sure. For usage as a noun, I think either (or "round trip") works.

content.body: Thanks, we usually go with hyphenless as English seems to trend towards that over time, so I guess I should make that consistent and add it to the style guide.
In the HTML repo, I'm puzzled by main~2 commit, which added 4 files to the repo?
